summary: In this 2020 interview, Robert Langer discusses his MIT lab's efforts to address the COVID-19 pandemic, including his role in co-founding Moderna. He explains the paradigm shift of mRNA vaccines, the importance of persistence in the face of scientific failure and public criticism, and the need for better global pandemic preparedness.

principles.statement: History and scientific results are the ultimate judges of your work, not contemporary criticism.
principles.rationale: Media and peers will often criticize new or unconventional ideas, but if you focus on doing the best science to help patients, the actual efficacy of the work will speak for itself over time.
principles.source_quote: sadly you get used to criticism in different ways, and you know, people can, you know, but history will ultimately decide what’s right and what isn’t.

principles.statement: Breakthrough science requires massive persistence and a willingness to fail repeatedly.
principles.rationale: Developing entirely new ways of drug delivery and tissue engineering involves thousands of experiments, the vast majority of which will be failures.
principles.source_quote: But we did thousands of experiments, and it did, it took a lot of persistence. Most of the time, of course, I’d fail.

principles.statement: The ultimate goal and metric of scientific research is whether it actually helps patients.
principles.rationale: Amidst media noise and skepticism, the only thing that truly matters is if the science works in patients and improves their lives.
principles.source_quote: ultimately to me, the answer will be, does it work or not? You know, in patients. Will it be useful for patients or not?

mental_models.name: The Body as a Factory
mental_models.description: Instead of using massive external manufacturing plants to produce therapeutic proteins (the classical vaccine approach), you can use mRNA to instruct the patient's own cells to manufacture the necessary proteins.
mental_models.example: Moderna's COVID-19 vaccine uses mRNA to make the patient's body produce the viral spike protein, bypassing the need for giant protein manufacturing plants and allowing for much faster development.

mental_models.name: Engineering Lenses on Biological Problems
mental_models.description: Viewing traditional medical or surgical challenges as problems that can be solved through chemical or engineering methods, such as novel drug delivery systems or new materials.
mental_models.example: Observing surgical problems in a hospital setting and realizing they could be solved by engineering new ways of drug delivery, using enzymes, or growing tissues.

heuristics: Focus on doing the best science you can; let history decide the rest.

signature_quotes.text: But we did thousands of experiments, and it did, it took a lot of persistence. Most of the time, of course, I’d fail.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the early days of developing his controlled-release drug delivery technology.

signature_quotes.text: by using the RNA, you’re able to have the body be the factory that makes the vaccine.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the paradigm shift of mRNA vaccines.

signature_quotes.text: sadly you get used to criticism in different ways, and you know, people can, you know, but history will ultimately decide what’s right and what isn’t.
signature_quotes.context: Responding to media criticism of Moderna's vaccine development process.

signature_quotes.text: And that’s all we can do is the best science we can do.
signature_quotes.context: On how his lab and companies handle the pressure and skepticism of developing new cures.

anti_patterns.description: Media sensationalism and agenda-driven criticism in science reporting.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates false controversies by cherry-picking anonymous quotes to fit a negative agenda, which confuses the public about what to believe rather than objectively evaluating if the science works.

anti_patterns.description: Under-investing in pandemic preparedness while over-investing in traditional military defense.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The greatest existential threats to humanity are often tiny microbes rather than traditional weapons, leaving society vastly unprepared for global tragedies.
